elements.sent-2411-001: The cosmological upper bound on the total neutrino mass is the dominant limit on this fundamental parameter.
elements.sent-2411-002: Recent observations---soon to be improved---have strongly tightened it, approaching the lower limit set by oscillation data.
elements.sent-2411-003: Understanding its physical origin, robustness, and model-independence becomes pressing.
elements.sent-2411-004: Here, we explicitly separate for the first time the two distinct cosmological neutrino-mass effects: the impact on background evolution, related to the energy in neutrino masses; and the ``kinematic'' impact on perturbations, related to neutrino free-streaming.
elements.sent-2411-005: We scrutinize how they affect CMB anisotropies, introducing two effective masses enclosing background ($\sum m_\nu^\mathrm{Backg.}$) and perturbations ($\sum m_\nu^\mathrm{Pert.}$) effects.
elements.sent-2411-006: We analyze CMB data, finding that the neutrino-mass bound is mostly a background measurement, i.e., how the neutrino energy density evolves with time.
elements.sent-2411-007: The bound on the ``kinematic'' variable $\sum m_\nu^\mathrm{Pert.}$ is largely relaxed, $\sum m_\nu^\mathrm{Pert.. < 0.8\,\mathrm{eV}$.}
elements.sent-2411-008: This work thus adds clarity to the physical origin of the cosmological neutrino-mass bound, which is mostly a measurement of the neutrino equation of state, providing also hints to evade such a bound.
elements.sent-2411-009: Neutrinos are hot thermal relics, permeating the Universe and being the most abundant particles after Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) photons.
elements.sent-2411-010: In particle physics, neutrino masses have meant the first departure from the Standard Model (SM), where no gauge-invariant renormalizable neutrino mass term can be written.
elements.sent-2411-011: Yet neutrino oscillation experiments have robustly measured two squared-mass differences, $|\Delta m^2_{31 |\equiv|m^2_3-m^2_1|\simeq 2.5\cdot 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{eV ^2$ and $\Delta m^2_{21 \equiv m^2_2-m^2_1 \simeq 7.5\cdot 10^{-5}\,\mathrm{eV ^2$~\cite{deSalas:2020pgw, Esteban:2024eli, Capozzi:2021fjo , confirming that neutrinos are massive and that the SM must be extended.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-015: Even if neutrino masses are tiny, determining them is a mandatory first step to understand not only the origin of the particle masses and hierarchies, but also the Universe's evolution and structure formation---neutrinos are, so far, the only known form of (hot) dark matter.
elements.sent-2411-016: In the early Universe, neutrinos are relativistic and behave as radiation, constituting up to $40\%$ of the total energy density.
elements.sent-2411-017: At temperatures $\lesssim \mathrm{MeV}$, their weak-interaction rate with the primordial plasma falls below the expansion rate of the Universe.
elements.sent-2411-018: Then, they decouple and stream freely, with their momentum being redshifted by the expansion.
elements.sent-2411-019: When neutrino momenta fall below their mass, neutrinos move at non-relativistic speeds, contributing to the Universe's matter energy density.
elements.sent-2411-020: Due to their abundance, neutrinos leave a measurable imprint on cosmological observables.
elements.sent-2411-021: This leads to upper bounds on the total neutrino mass that are around $\sum m_\nu \lesssim 0.05$--$0.3$\,eV~\cite{DESI:2024mwx, DESI:2024hhd, Jiang:2024viw, Wang:2024hen , potentially raising a tension between oscillation results and cosmological observations.}

elements.sent-2411-025: To clarify the robustness of cosmological neutrino-mass determinations, and the complementarity with direct searches, it is key to understand the physical effects of neutrino masses that cosmology is most sensitive to~\cite{Lesgourgues:2013sjj .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Is current and upcoming data only sensitive to the background energy in neutrino masses, degenerate with other sources of energy and cosmological unknowns? Or is it sensitive to a characteristic scale-dependent imprint, induced by neutrinos not moving at the speed of light? In this paper, we explicitly separate background neutrino-mass effects, which capture the evolution of the average neutrino energy density; from perturbations effects, which capture ``kinematic'' scale-dependent effects directly related to the neutrino speed.
elements.sent-2411-027: As a first step, we focus on their impact on CMB anisotropies, leaving other observables for future work~\cite{LSSfuture .}
elements.sent-2411-028: To gain intuition, we study in detail the physical origin of detectable effects.
elements.sent-2411-029: Finally, we carry out an analysis of current CMB data, finding out that it mostly constraints background neutrino-mass effects.
elements.sent-2411-030: In turn, the limit on perturbations effects is significantly weaker than the standard bounds.
elements.sent-2411-031: Our results provide insight into the physical origin of cosmological neutrino-mass bounds, and serve as a benchmark of extended models that could affect them.
elements.sent-2411-032: As one direct consequence, we find that any change on the background expansion history that is degenerate with the impact of neutrino masses can strongly affect the cosmological neutrino-mass determination.
elements.sent-2411-033: Our definitions of background and perturbations effects are not tied to particular observables (e.g., CMB anisotropies are perturbations, but their evolution is sensitive to the background expansion rate), providing a generic framework to understand the cosmological effects of neutrino masses.
elements.sent-2411-034: The structure of the manuscript is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-035: In \cref{sec:formalism , we describe our formalism to separate background and perturbations effects.}
elements.sent-2411-036: In \cref{sec:phenomenology , we examine and illustrate their physical effects on CMB anisotropies at different angular scales.}
elements.sent-2411-037: In \cref{sec:results , we present our statistical analysis together with the cosmological constraints on the key parameters of this study.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Finally, we draw our conclusions in \cref{sec:conclusions .}
elements.sent-2411-039: In the Appendices, we provide further explanations and results.
elements.sent-2411-040: After decoupling from the primordial plasma, neutrinos only affect cosmological observables via gravity, i.e., via their stress-energy tensor $T^\mu_\nu$ that enters the Einstein equations.
elements.sent-2411-041: In this Section, we set up the formalism to separate background and perturbations effects.
elements.sent-2411-042: We start from the most general parametrization, and compute the relevant quantities for the specific case of massive, non-interacting neutrinos.
elements.sent-2411-043: On top of establishing our framework, this identifies the neutrino-mass effects that cosmology can be sensitive to, enabling a better understanding of the cosmological neutrino-mass determination.
elements.sent-2411-044: At the background level, the Einstein equations lead to the first Friedmann equation \begin{equation}\label{eq:Friedmann} \left(\frac{a'}{a}\right)^2 = \frac{8\pi G}{3} a^2 (\rho_\gamma+\rho_\mathrm{b}+\rho_\mathrm{cdm}+\rho_\nu+\rho_\Lambda)\, ,\end{equation} with primes derivatives with respect to conformal time, $a$ the scale factor, $G$ Newton's constant, and $\rho_i$ the background energy density of the species $i$ (we include photons, baryons, cold dark matter, neutrinos and a cosmological constant).
elements.sent-2411-045: This is the only equation where neutrinos modify the background evolution of the Universe.

elements.section-2411-008: Detailed physical effects on the CMB anisotropies

elements.sent-2411-253: To gain physical intuition, it is customary to write $\Theta_{\gamma \ell}$ in terms of a line-of-sight integral~\cite{Zaldarriaga:1995gi \begin{equation}\label{eq:los} \Theta_{\gamma \ell}\left(\eta_0, k\right)=\int_{\eta_{\text {in }}}^{\eta_0} \left\{g(\eta)\left(\Theta_{\gamma 0}+\psi\right)+\left[g(\eta) k^{-2} \theta_{\mathrm{B}}\right]^{\prime}+e^{-\tau}\left(\phi^{\prime} +\psi^{\prime}\right)\right\} j_\ell\left[k\left(\eta_0-\eta\right)\right]\mathrm{d} \eta\, ,\end{equation} with $\eta_\mathrm{in}$ the initial conformal time, $\theta_B$ the baryon velocity divergence, \begin{equation}\tau(\eta) \equiv \int_{\eta}^{\eta_0} a(\eta') n_e(\eta') \sigma_\mathrm{T}\,\mathrm{d}\eta'\end{equation} the photon optical depth, which changes from 0 well before recombination to 1 after recombination; and $g(\eta) \equiv [e^{-\tau(\eta) ]'$ the visibility function, which is sharply peaked around recombination~\cite{Lesgourgues:2013sjj .}}}
elements.sent-2411-255: The first term can be understood as the temperature anisotropy at recombination, redshifted by the local gravitational potential (Sachs-Wolfe effect, SW); the second term as a Doppler shift due to baryon velocities at recombination; and the third term as the accumulated gravitational redshift as CMB photons travels from recombination to us (Integrated Sachs-Wolfe effect, ISW).

elements.subsection-2411-008: Physical effects on the SW contribution
elements.sent-2411-281: The SW effect accounts for temperature anisotropies at recombination, redshifted by the local gravitational potential.
elements.sent-2411-282: We now break down the different physical effects of $\sum m_\nu^\mathrm{Backg.}$ and $\sum m_\nu^\mathrm{Pert.}$ on the SW contribution in~\cref{fig:SW-ISW .}
elements.sent-2411-283: Low multipoles ($\ell \lesssim 10$) correspond to modes that are super-horizon at recombination.
elements.sent-2411-284: Their evolution only depends on background quantities, and it can be analytically estimated.
elements.sent-2411-285: Setting $k\ll \frac{a' {a}$ in \cref{eq:our-sw , the $\ell \to 0$ plateau is given by~\cite{Dodelson:2020abc \begin{equation}\lim_{\ell \to 0}\ell(\ell+1)C_\ell^{\mathrm{SW}} \approx 8\left[\frac{1+3w_\mathrm{tot}(\eta_{\mathrm{rec}})}{5+3w_\mathrm{tot}(\eta_{\mathrm{rec}})}\right]^2 A_s\, , \label{eq:SW_plateau}\end{equation} which coincides with the standard result $\ell(\ell+1)C_\ell^{\mathrm{SW }\approx 8A_s/25$ for matter domination, $w_{\mathrm{tot (\eta_{\mathrm{rec ) = 0$~\cite{Sachs:1967er,Dodelson:2020abc .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-286: Increasing $\sum m_\nu^\mathrm{Backg.}$ reduces the neutrino contribution to $w_\mathrm{tot}$, reducing the $\ell \to 0$ limit of the SW contribution.
elements.sent-2411-287: There is also a sub-percent effect of $\sum m_\nu^\mathrm{Pert.}$ that, as explained above, is due to the corresponding modes not being completely out of the horizon at recombination.
elements.sent-2411-288: Intermediate multipoles ($10 \lesssim \ell \lesssim 500$) correspond to modes that are comparable to the horizon at recombination.
elements.sent-2411-289: Their evolution is largely influenced by the gravitational potentials $\phi$ and $\psi$.
elements.sent-2411-290: It can be approximated as~\cite{Hu:1995kot \begin{equation}\label{eq:theta_gamma_oscillator} m_{\mathrm{eff}}\Theta_{\gamma 0}'' + k^2 \frac{\Theta_{\gamma 0}}{3}\simeq - m_{\mathrm{eff}}\left(k^2\frac{\psi}{3}-\phi''\right)\, ,\end{equation} where $m_{\mathrm{eff \equiv 1 + \frac{\rho_b + P_b {\rho_\gamma + P_\gamma}\simeq 1+ 3\rho_b/(4\rho_\gamma)$; with $\rho_b$, $P_b$, $\rho_\gamma$, and $P_\gamma$ the baryon energy density, baryon pressure, photon energy density, and photon pressure, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-291: Decaying gravitational potentials enhance the amplitude of oscillations via \cref{eq:theta_gamma_oscillator .}
elements.sent-2411-292: Physically, large initial potentials force the fluid into a highly compressed state.
elements.sent-2411-293: If they then decay, photon pressure overcomes gravity and the photon-baryon fluid oscillates with a larger amplitude.
elements.sent-2411-294: On top of that, decaying gravitational potentials reduce the redshift experienced by CMB photons as they leave the last-scattering surface, further increasing the anisotropies.
elements.sent-2411-295: For a detailed explanation of these effects, we refer to the work of Ref.~\cite{Hu:1995kot .}

elements.subsection-2411-009: Physical effects on the ISW contribution
elements.sent-2411-309: The ISW contribution accounts for the accumulated gravitational redshift of CMB photons on their way to Earth.
elements.sent-2411-310: Physically, when photons enter gravitational potential wells they get redshifted, and when they exit them they get blueshifted.
elements.sent-2411-311: If the depth of the gravitational potential wells is constant, both effects compensate each other.
elements.sent-2411-312: A nonzero ISW effect thus requires time-dependent gravitational potentials.
elements.sent-2411-313: This can only be the case if the Universe is not matter-dominated~\cite{Dodelson:2020abc .}
elements.sent-2411-314: The ISW effect is only relevant at multipoles $\ell \lesssim 200$, as shown in the top right panel of \cref{fig:SW-ISW .}
elements.sent-2411-315: At smaller scales, gravitational potentials experience a long period of radiation domination inside the horizon, and they are suppressed.
elements.sent-2411-316: It can be split into an early and a late contribution.
elements.sent-2411-317: The early contribution is due to the residual radiation left after recombination, and it affects modes that were inside the horizon at that time, $10 \lesssim \ell \lesssim 200$.
elements.sent-2411-318: As discussed above (see \cref{fig:psi-decay ), background neutrino masses boost the decay of gravitational potentials and perturbations masses slow it down.}
elements.sent-2411-319: Therefore, $\sum m_\nu^\mathrm{Backg.}$ and $\sum m_\nu^\mathrm{Pert.}$ produce an enhancement and a depletion of the ISW effect, respectively; as can be seen in \cref{fig:SW-ISW .}
elements.sent-2411-320: The late contribution is due to the late-time epoch of accelerated expansion caused by the cosmological constant $\Lambda$, and it affects modes that entered the horizon recently, $\ell \lesssim 10$.
elements.sent-2411-321: As explained in \cref{sec:CMB in the main text, the main neutrino-mass effect is indirect: the angular scale of CMB peaks $\theta_s$, \cref{eq:theta_s , is very well measured, and $\sum m_\nu^\mathrm{Backg.}$ would modify it by changing the expansion rate of the Universe.}}
elements.sent-2411-322: To compensate for this effect, $H_0$ gets reduced, reducing $\Lambda$ and the late ISW effect as can be seen in \cref{fig:SW-ISW .}
elements.sent-2411-323: There is also a subleading reduction caused by $\sum m_\nu^\mathrm{Pert.}$, as neutrino clustering slows down the $\Lambda$-induced decay of potentials.
elements.sent-2411-324: This effect is small, see \cref{fig:cmb1,fig:SW-ISW , and it is only present if $\sum m_\nu^{\mathrm{Backg.}}}
elements.sent-2411-325: On their way to Earth, CMB photons get randomly deflected by the gravitational pull of the large-scale structure of the Universe~\cite{Dodelson:2020abc,Lewis:2006fu .}
elements.sent-2411-326: This weak gravitational lensing is a second-order non-linear effect, since it is a perturbative deflection of perturbative anisotropies, but with the precision of current data it is detectable.
elements.sent-2411-327: Lensing has two observable features on CMB anisotropies, that are more prominent at high multipoles.
elements.sent-2411-328: First, it smoothes out the power spectrum, since it mixes photons coming from different points in the last scattering surface.
elements.sent-2411-329: Second, it transfers power from large scales to small scales, due to its non-linear nature.
elements.sent-2411-330: This leads to increased anisotropies at high $\ell$.
elements.sent-2411-331: Neutrino masses affect the evolution of gravitational potentials, as explained above and shown in~\cref{fig:psi-decay .}
elements.sent-2411-333: On the one hand, background neutrino masses increase the expansion rate of the Universe and boost the decay of gravitational potentials.
elements.sent-2411-335: On the other hand, perturbations masses make neutrinos cluster below the free-streaming scale and slow down the decay of potentials.
elements.sent-2411-336: This enhances CMB lensing in a scale-dependent way.
